question_text: The centripetal force required to keep a body moving in a circular path is directed:
explanation: Centripetal force is always directed towards the centre of the circular path. It is this inward force that continuously changes the direction of velocity, keeping the body on its circular trajectory. Without this inward force, the body would move in a straight line (tangent to the circle).
subject: physics

options.text: Towards the centre of the circular path
Away from the centre of the circular path
Along the tangent to the circular path
At 45° to the radius of the circular path

elimination_hints.hint: A force directed away from the centre would push the body outward, not keep it moving in a circle. The so-called 'centrifugal force' is a pseudo force in rotating frames, not a real force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing centripetal force with centrifugal force

elimination_hints.hint: A tangential force would change the speed of the body, not its direction. Centripetal force changes direction to maintain circular motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tangential force with centripetal force

elimination_hints.hint: Centripetal force is strictly radially inward (along the radius toward the centre), not at any angle to the radius.

question_text: The centripetal acceleration of a body moving with speed v in a circle of radius r is:
explanation: The centripetal acceleration is given by a_c = v²/r, directed towards the centre of the circular path. This can also be written as a_c = ω²r, where ω is the angular velocity. It represents the rate of change of direction of velocity.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: v/r has dimensions of 1/s (angular velocity), not m/s² (acceleration). The correct expression squares the velocity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing angular velocity with centripetal acceleration

elimination_hints.hint: v²r has dimensions of m³/s², which is not the dimension of acceleration. The radius should be in the denominator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing radius in numerator instead of denominator

elimination_hints.hint: vr² has dimensions of m³/s, which is not acceleration. The correct formula is v²/r.

question_text: A stone tied to a string is whirled in a horizontal circle. If the string breaks, the stone will:
explanation: When the string breaks, the centripetal force (tension) ceases to act. By Newton's first law, the stone will continue moving in the direction of its velocity at the instant the string breaks, which is along the tangent to the circle at that point.
subject: physics

options.text: Move radially outward from the centre
Move radially inward toward the centre
Fly off tangentially to the circle
Continue moving in the circular path

elimination_hints.hint: There is no outward force on the stone. The velocity is tangential, not radial, so the stone moves tangentially, not radially outward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing centrifugal force pushes objects outward when released

elimination_hints.hint: Moving inward would require an inward force, but the tension (the only inward force) no longer exists after the string breaks.
Circular motion requires a continuous centripetal force. Without the string providing tension, there is no force to maintain the circular path.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking circular motion continues without centripetal force

question_text: In uniform circular motion, the quantity that remains constant is:
explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed (magnitude of velocity) remains constant, but the direction of velocity continuously changes. Since velocity is a vector (has both magnitude and direction), the velocity itself is not constant. Acceleration and force are also not constant in direction.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Velocity is a vector quantity. Although its magnitude (speed) is constant, its direction changes continuously in circular motion, so velocity is not constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing speed (scalar) with velocity (vector)

elimination_hints.hint: Centripetal acceleration has constant magnitude (v²/r) but its direction changes continuously as it always points toward the centre.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that acceleration is a vector whose direction matters

elimination_hints.hint: The centripetal force has constant magnitude but continuously changes direction (always toward the centre), so it is not a constant vector.

question_text: A car of mass 1000 kg is moving on a flat circular road of radius 50 m. The maximum speed at which the car can take the turn safely without skidding is: (Coefficient of friction μ = 0.5, g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: On a flat (unbanked) circular road, friction provides the centripetal force. At the maximum speed, mv²/r = μmg. Solving: v² = μgr = 0.5 × 10 × 50 = 250, so v = √250 ≈ 15.8 m/s. The mass cancels out and does not affect the maximum safe speed.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 10 m/s gives v² = 100, requiring μgr = 100. But μgr = 250, so the car can safely go faster than 10 m/s.
22.4 m/s gives v² = 500. This would require μ = 1.0 for the given road, which is twice the actual coefficient of friction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using incorrect coefficient or forgetting to take square root properly

elimination_hints.hint: 25 m/s gives v² = 625. The centripetal force needed (12,500 N) would exceed the maximum friction (5,000 N), causing the car to skid.

question_text: A cyclist goes around a circular track of radius 100 m at a speed of 20 m/s. The angle at which the cyclist should lean with the vertical is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: For a cyclist or vehicle on a circular track, the lean angle θ satisfies tan θ = v²/(rg) = (20)²/(100 × 10) = 400/1000 = 0.4. Therefore θ = tan⁻¹(0.4) ≈ 21.8° ≈ 22°. This lean provides the necessary centripetal force component.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: tan⁻¹(0.2) would require v²/(rg) = 0.2, meaning v² = 200 or v ≈ 14.1 m/s, not 20 m/s.
tan⁻¹(0.3) doesn't match v²/(rg) = 400/1000 = 0.4. Check your arithmetic.
tan⁻¹(0.5) would require v² = 500, corresponding to v ≈ 22.4 m/s, not 20 m/s.

question_text: A road is banked at an angle θ for a curve of radius r. The maximum safe speed for a vehicle on this banked road (ignoring friction) is:
explanation: On a frictionless banked road, the horizontal component of the normal force provides the centripetal force: N sin θ = mv²/r and N cos θ = mg. Dividing: tan θ = v²/(rg), so v = √(rg tan θ). This is the optimum speed for the banked road without friction.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Using sin θ alone doesn't account for the vertical equilibrium condition. The correct derivation involves dividing the horizontal and vertical force equations, yielding tan θ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using sin instead of tan in the banking formula

elimination_hints.hint: cos θ appears in the vertical equilibrium (N cos θ = mg) but the speed formula involves tan θ, not cos θ.
This is the reciprocal of the correct expression inside the square root. tan θ should be in the numerator, not the denominator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the tan θ relationship

question_text: A conical pendulum has a string of length L making an angle θ with the vertical. The speed of the bob of mass m in the horizontal circle is:
explanation: For a conical pendulum, the radius of the circle is r = L sin θ. The tension component T sin θ provides centripetal force, and T cos θ = mg. From these: v² = rg tan θ = (L sin θ)(g)(sin θ/cos θ) = gL sin²θ/cos θ. Equivalently, v = √(gL sin θ tan θ) = √(gL sin²θ/cos θ).
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: √(gL cos θ) is the expression that appears in the time period formula of a conical pendulum (T = 2π√(L cos θ/g)), not in the speed formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing time period formula with speed formula for conical pendulum

elimination_hints.hint: √(gL sin θ) only accounts for the radius r = L sin θ but misses the tan θ factor from the force balance.
√(gL tan θ) would be correct if the radius were L, but the radius is L sin θ, not L.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using string length instead of radius of circular path

question_text: A body of mass 0.5 kg is whirled in a vertical circle of radius 2 m. The minimum speed at the topmost point of the circle so that the string does not become slack is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: At the topmost point, both the weight mg and the tension T provide centripetal force: mg + T = mv²/r. For the string to not go slack, T ≥ 0. At minimum speed, T = 0, so mg = mv²_min/r, giving v_min = √(gr) = √(10 × 2) = √20 ≈ 4.47 m/s.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: √40 would give v² = 40, implying gr = 40 or r = 4 m with g = 10. But the radius is 2 m, so gr = 20.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubling the product gr incorrectly

elimination_hints.hint: √10 gives v² = 10, which means mv²/r = 0.5 × 10/2 = 2.5 N, less than mg = 5 N. Gravity alone would exceed the centripetal force needed, and the string would go slack.
√50 corresponds to √(5gr), which is the minimum speed at the bottom of a vertical circle, not at the top.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing minimum speed at top with minimum speed at bottom

question_text: A car is moving on a banked road of angle 30° with radius 100 m. The optimum speed of the car (at which no friction is needed) is approximately: (Take g = 10 m/s², tan 30° = 0.577)
explanation: The optimum speed on a banked road (where no friction is needed) is v = √(rg tan θ) = √(100 × 10 × 0.577) = √577 ≈ 24 m/s. At this speed, the horizontal component of the normal force exactly provides the required centripetal force.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 14 m/s gives v² = 196. The required rg tan θ = 577, so 14 m/s is too slow for the banking angle.
18 m/s gives v² = 324, still less than 577. This car would tend to slide inward on the banked road.
31 m/s gives v² = 961, much greater than 577. At this speed, the car would tend to slide outward off the road.

question_text: A ball of mass m is attached to a string and whirled in a vertical circle of radius r. The minimum speed at the bottom of the circle for the ball to complete the full vertical circle is:
explanation: At the top of the circle, minimum speed v_top = √(gr). Using energy conservation from bottom to top: ½mv²_bottom = ½mv²_top + mg(2r). Substituting v_top = √(gr): ½v²_bottom = ½gr + 2gr = 5gr/2. So v_bottom = √(5gr). This ensures the string stays taut throughout the entire loop.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: √(gr) is the minimum speed at the top of the circle, not the bottom. By the time the ball reaches the top from the bottom, it needs additional speed to overcome gravity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing critical speed at top with critical speed at bottom

elimination_hints.hint: √(2gr) would only account for the height gain of 2r using ½v² = g(2r), but it ignores the kinetic energy needed at the top to maintain the circular path.
elimination_hints.misconception: Only considering potential energy change, not the kinetic energy requirement at the top

elimination_hints.hint: √(3gr) results from an incorrect energy balance. The correct balance gives v²_bottom = 5gr, not 3gr.

question_text: A motorcyclist rides inside a vertical cylindrical wall (death well) of radius 5 m. The minimum speed required so that the rider does not fall is: (Coefficient of friction between tyres and wall μ = 0.5, g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: In a death well, the normal force from the wall provides centripetal force: N = mv²/r. The frictional force (upward) supports the weight: μN ≥ mg, so μ(mv²/r) ≥ mg, giving v² ≥ rg/μ = 5 × 10/0.5 = 100. Therefore v_min = 10 m/s.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: At v = 5 m/s, N = mv²/r = m × 25/5 = 5m. Friction = μN = 0.5 × 5m = 2.5m < mg = 10m. The rider would slide down.
15 m/s gives v² = 225 > 100, so the rider is safe but this is not the minimum speed. The minimum is when friction exactly balances weight.
20 m/s is well above the minimum. While safe, the question asks for the minimum speed to avoid falling.

question_text:
Assertion (A): On a banked road, a vehicle can negotiate a curve without any friction at a particular speed.
Reason (R): At the optimum speed, the horizontal component of the normal force provides the necessary centripetal force.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A. When a road is banked at angle θ, the normal force has a horizontal component N sin θ that points toward the centre of the curve. At the optimum speed v = √(rg tan θ), this horizontal component exactly equals mv²/r, so no friction is needed to negotiate the curve.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains the Assertion: the horizontal component of the normal force on a banked road is the mechanism by which friction is eliminated at the optimum speed.
The Reason is a correct physics statement. On a banked road, N sin θ indeed provides centripetal force when friction is absent.
The Assertion is correct. Banked roads are specifically designed so that vehicles can safely turn at the design speed without depending on friction.

question_text:
Assertion (A): In a vertical circular motion with a string, the tension at the bottom of the circle is maximum.
Reason (R): At the bottom, the centripetal force is provided by the difference of tension and weight.
explanation: The Assertion is correct: tension is maximum at the bottom. However, the Reason is incorrect in its statement. At the bottom, the net centripetal force equation is T − mg = mv²/r, so T = mv²/r + mg. The centripetal force is provided by the net inward force, which is the excess of tension over weight (T − mg), not 'the difference of tension and weight' as a standalone centripetal force. Actually, re-reading: R says centripetal force = T − mg, which is correct. Wait: at the bottom, the centre is above, so T acts upward (toward centre) and mg acts downward (away from centre). Net inward = T − mg = mv²/r. R states this correctly. So both are correct and R explains A? T = mv²/r + mg shows T is large at the bottom because both terms add. At the top, T = mv²/r − mg, so T is smaller. But does R explain why T is maximum? R correctly states the force equation at the bottom. Since T = mv²/r + mg (both terms positive, and v is maximum at bottom due to energy conservation), T is indeed maximum. But R only states the force balance, not the complete argument about maximum speed. Still, R is the core reason. Let me reconsider: A is correct. R is correct. R explains A because T = mv²/r + mg means tension equals centripetal force plus weight, making it maximum at the bottom. Answer should be A.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A: since T − mg = mv²/r at the bottom, T = mv²/r + mg. The tension must support both the weight and provide centripetal force, making it maximum.
The Reason correctly states the force equation at the bottom of a vertical circle. The centripetal acceleration is provided by T − mg.
The Assertion is correct. At the bottom of a vertical circle, the speed is maximum (by energy conservation) and the force equation gives T = mv²/r + mg, the largest value of tension.

question_text:
Match the following positions in a vertical circle (Column I) with the expressions for tension/normal force in the string/track (Column II), where v is the speed at that point:

Column I:
A. Top of the circle (string)
B. Bottom of the circle (string)
C. Horizontal position (string, at the side)
D. Top of a circular loop on a track (normal force from track)

Column II:
1. T = mv²/r + mg
2. T = mv²/r − mg
3. T = mv²/r
4. N = mv²/r − mg
explanation: A-2: At the top with a string, T + mg = mv²/r, so T = mv²/r − mg. B-1: At the bottom, T − mg = mv²/r, so T = mv²/r + mg. C-3: At the horizontal position, weight acts perpendicular to the radius (downward) and doesn't contribute to centripetal direction, so T = mv²/r (tension alone provides centripetal force). D-4: At the top of a loop track, N + mg = mv²/r (both N and mg point toward centre), so N = mv²/r − mg.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps top and bottom formulas. At the top, weight aids centripetal direction (both point to centre), so T = mv²/r − mg (subtract), not + mg.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the tension formulas at top and bottom

elimination_hints.hint: C-4 is incorrect because at the horizontal position for a string, the weight is perpendicular to the radius and T = mv²/r (not mv²/r − mg).
This gives entirely wrong assignments. At the top (A), the formula involves subtraction of mg, not just N = mv²/r − mg (which is for the track).

question_text:
Match the following circular motion scenarios (Column I) with the source of centripetal force (Column II):

Column I:
A. Car on a flat circular road
B. Car on a banked road (no friction)
C. Electron orbiting a nucleus
D. Satellite orbiting Earth

Column II:
1. Gravitational force
2. Electrostatic (Coulomb) force
3. Friction between tyres and road
4. Horizontal component of normal force
explanation: A-3: On a flat road, friction between tyres and road provides centripetal force. B-4: On a frictionless banked road, the horizontal component of the normal force (N sin θ) provides centripetal force. C-2: The electrostatic (Coulomb) force between the electron and nucleus provides centripetal force. D-1: Gravitational force between the satellite and Earth provides centripetal force for orbital motion.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: A-4 says the normal force provides centripetal force on a flat road. On a flat road, the normal force is vertical and cannot provide horizontal centripetal force; friction does.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing normal force role on flat vs banked roads

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps electron (Coulomb force) and satellite (gravitational force). Electrons are held in orbit by electrostatic force, not gravity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the forces governing atomic and planetary orbits

elimination_hints.hint: B-1 incorrectly assigns gravitational force to banked roads. Gravity acts vertically and is balanced by N cos θ; it's the horizontal component N sin θ that provides centripetal force.

question_text: Banking of roads is done to reduce the dependence on friction for negotiating curved paths.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Banking a road tilts the road surface inward, so the normal force has a horizontal component that contributes to the centripetal force. This reduces (or at the optimum speed, completely eliminates) the need for friction to provide centripetal force, making the curve safer especially in wet conditions.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is correct. The primary purpose of banking is to use the normal force component for centripetal force, reducing reliance on friction.
This option is not applicable.
This option is not applicable.

question_text: Centripetal force is a new type of force, separate from forces like gravity, friction, and tension.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. Centripetal force is not a new type of force. It is the name given to the net force (or a component of force) that is directed toward the centre of the circular path. The actual force can be gravity (as in planetary orbits), tension (as in a string), friction (as on roads), normal force, or any combination of these.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Centripetal force is not a fundamental force. It is a label for whichever force (or component of forces) acts radially inward in circular motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking centripetal force is a separate fundamental force

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable.
This option is not applicable.

question_text: For a car moving on a banked road of angle θ without friction, the optimum speed is given by v = √(_____). If the speed exceeds this value, the car tends to slide _____ on the banked road.
explanation: The optimum speed on a frictionless banked road is v = √(rg tan θ), derived from the balance of the horizontal component of normal force with the centripetal force requirement. If the car exceeds this speed, the required centripetal force exceeds what the normal force component can provide, and the car tends to slide outward (up the bank), away from the centre of the curve.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The formula uses tan θ, not sin θ. Also, exceeding the optimum speed causes outward sliding, not inward.
The formula rg tan θ is correct, but the sliding direction is wrong. At speeds above optimum, the car needs more centripetal force than available and slides outward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of sliding when speed exceeds optimum

elimination_hints.hint: The formula should be rg tan θ, not rg cos θ. Although the outward sliding direction is correct, the formula is wrong.

question_text:
An amusement park ride consists of a vertical loop of radius 10 m. A car of mass 500 kg enters the loop from the bottom and must complete the full loop without losing contact with the track.

What is the minimum speed the car must have at the top of the loop, and what is the corresponding normal force from the track on the car at the top? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: At the top of the loop, both the weight (mg) and normal force (N) act downward (toward the centre). The equation is N + mg = mv²/r. For minimum speed, N = 0 (the car barely maintains contact). So mg = mv²_min/r, giving v_min = √(gr) = √(10 × 10) = 10 m/s. At this minimum speed, the normal force N = 0.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The speed is correct at 10 m/s, but N = 5000 N would mean mv²/r = N + mg = 5000 + 5000 = 10000, giving v² = 200 and v ≈ 14.1 m/s, which is not the minimum speed condition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing minimum speed condition with the case where N equals mg

elimination_hints.hint: √50 ≈ 7.07 m/s is less than √(gr) = 10 m/s. At this speed, the required centripetal force would be less than mg alone, so the car would lose contact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using v = √(gr/2) instead of v = √(gr)

elimination_hints.hint: If N = 500 N at v = 10 m/s, then N + mg = 500 + 5000 = 5500 N, but mv²/r = 500 × 100/10 = 5000 N. These don't match, so this is inconsistent.
